men, too, lie in the same district. or
course we believe this road will be a
money maker. Now, in order that
the company might commence busi-
ness it was necessary that the entire
capital stock should be subscribed
for This Mr.. Twohy, Mr.. Coolidge,
have done With us, as l have said,
will be our associates in business
but we want, and must have, the peo-
pie of the communities in which we
operate to be large stockholders in
the road. The reason for this is
twofold: In the first place. we want
every person along the road to feel
that 1t is his road, that he has an
interest in it, and that every dollars
worth of business he can turn to it
is so much money in his pocket. a
community of interest between rail-
road and it's patrons, under which
each must assist the other If he
would himself profit, can but rebound
to the advantage of both. Again,
if the people where the road runs
own it, they can dictate it's policy.
There will never then be use to
seek from the legislature relief
against it. If it's rates are not just
and fair, the remedy lies in their own
hands. A locally owned road will
never deal unjustly with local people.
In the second place, this enterprise
is too big for ourselves and our as
sociates to put through alone. Such
from s3.O00,000 to s4,000,000 to put
in operation. If the people here do
not assist us by purchasing stock
and aiding us in getting right of way,
we shall either be forced to endeavor
to secure eastern capital, or else li
in our project. The first we do not
desire to do, even if it were possible
to raise the funds we need, since that
would put the road on the same basis
as other roads now operating here,
viz: controlled by men who have no
other interest in the country than to
take rs much out of 1t as they can.
The other horn of the dilemma is even
worse. 1t means to us not only the
loss of the money we have and shall
put into the project before we will
abandon it, but also serious blow
to our interests and to those of every
other person in this community"
